_meta.authoringNotes: Numbers checked against AAO 2024 AMD Preferred Practice Pattern (PMID 39918524), AREDS2 (PMID 23644932), CATT (PMID 21526923), Wong et al Lancet Global Health 2014 (PMID 25104651). Drug doses cross-verified against UpToDate Lexicomp.

h1Title: Age-Related Macular Degeneration: Symptoms, Causes & Treatment
heroOverview: Age-related macular degeneration (AMD) is the leading cause of irreversible central vision loss in adults over 50 in high-income countries. It damages the macula, the small central part of the retina responsible for sharp straight-ahead vision used for reading, driving, and recognizing faces. Roughly 200 million people worldwide live with some form of AMD, with prevalence rising steeply after age 70. Dry AMD (about 85% of cases) progresses slowly over years through drusen accumulation and retinal pigment epithelium atrophy, while wet AMD (about 15%) can erase central vision within weeks if untreated. Intravitreal anti-VEGF injections have transformed wet AMD outcomes, and new complement inhibitors now slow geographic atrophy in advanced dry disease.

keyStats.prevalence: Roughly 200 million people worldwide; ~20 million US adults have some form of AMD (CDC 2019)
keyStats.demographics: Strongly age-related — prevalence rises from 2% at age 50-59 to over 30% by age 80
keyStats.avgAge: Most diagnoses occur after age 60; advanced AMD is uncommon before 65
keyStats.globalCases: Projected 288 million worldwide by 2040 (Wong et al, Lancet Global Health 2014)

definition: Age-related macular degeneration (ICD-10: H35.30) is a progressive degenerative disease of the central retina that targets the macula, the 5.5 mm region containing the cone photoreceptors responsible for high-acuity central vision. Disease starts with abnormal accumulation of lipid and protein deposits called drusen between the retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) and Bruch's membrane, driven by chronic oxidative stress and dysregulated complement activation. Early AMD is defined by medium drusen without pigmentary change; intermediate AMD by large drusen or RPE abnormalities; and late AMD by either geographic atrophy (well-demarcated patches of RPE and photoreceptor loss) or neovascular (wet) disease, in which abnormal choroidal vessels invade through Bruch's membrane and leak fluid or blood under the retina. Vision loss is central and irreversible once photoreceptors die — peripheral vision is preserved, so AMD does not cause total blindness. The condition is managed by retina specialists working with general ophthalmologists and optometrists, and increasingly involves low-vision rehabilitation specialists as functional impairment progresses.

typesClassification.description: Medium-sized drusen (63-125 µm) without pigment abnormalities. Vision usually normal; 5-year progression to late AMD is low (around 1.3% in AREDS data).
Large drusen (>125 µm) or pigment changes. 5-year risk of progression to late AMD is roughly 18%; AREDS2 supplements are indicated at this stage.
Well-demarcated areas of RPE and photoreceptor loss. Progresses at about 1.5-2.6 mm² per year. Central vision is lost when atrophy involves the fovea.
Abnormal choroidal neovascularization causes subretinal fluid, hemorrhage, and rapid central vision loss. Without anti-VEGF therapy, mean visual acuity drops by 3-4 lines within 2 years.

primarySymptoms: Gradual blurring of central vision that does not improve with new glasses — straight-ahead detail (reading text, recognizing faces) becomes harder while peripheral vision remains intact.
Straight lines appearing wavy or distorted (metamorphopsia) — door frames bend, the centerline on a road curves, words on a page seem to bow. This is the hallmark warning sign of wet AMD.
A blurred or dark spot in the center of vision (central scotoma) that follows the gaze and obscures whatever the patient looks at directly.
Difficulty adapting to low light and slow recovery from glare — entering a dimly lit restaurant or driving at dusk becomes noticeably harder than it used to be.
Reduced color brightness — colors look washed out, less saturated, or different between the two eyes when compared one eye at a time.
Needing brighter and brighter light for reading or fine tasks, often more than what corrects a normal age-related lens change.
Words on a page appearing to have missing letters or pieces because the central scotoma blocks parts of each line.
Sudden vision loss in one eye over days to weeks — typical of wet AMD activation and a same-week ophthalmology indication.

Charles Bonnet visual hallucinations (seeing patterns, faces, or scenes that are not there) in advanced AMD as the brain fills in missing visual information — these are not psychiatric and do not indicate dementia.
earlyWarningSigns: Subtle distortion noticed only when one eye is covered — drivers often discover this when comparing left and right eye vision at a stoplight
A specific spot on an Amsler grid where lines look wavy or missing — small daily changes are the earliest signal of wet conversion
Increasing difficulty reading small print under normal indoor light despite up-to-date glasses
Slower-than-usual eye adjustment when walking from sunlight into a building or vice versa
emergencySigns: Sudden onset of central distortion, dark spot, or vision loss in one eye over hours or days — requires retina specialist review within 24-72 hours to rule out new wet AMD
Sudden showers of new floaters or flashing lights with central vision loss — may signal retinal detachment, a separate ophthalmologic emergency
Loss of all vision in one eye (including peripheral) is NOT typical of AMD — evaluate urgently for retinal artery occlusion, stroke, or optic neuropathy
Rapid worsening over a few days in a patient already on anti-VEGF therapy — possible breakthrough exudation or treatment failure requiring earlier injection

causes.cause: Age-related oxidative damage to the retinal pigment epithelium
causes.description: Photoreceptor outer segments turn over daily and are phagocytosed by RPE cells. Over decades this generates lipofuscin and oxidized lipid byproducts that accumulate faster than the RPE can clear, leading to drusen formation and cellular dysfunction.

Genome-wide studies have linked common variants in complement factor H (CFH Y402H), C3, and CFB to a 2-7 fold rise in AMD risk. Chronic low-grade complement activation drives drusen biogenesis, RPE injury, and progression to geographic atrophy.

Hypoxia and VEGF upregulation in the aging macula stimulate abnormal new vessels from the choriocapillaris to invade through Bruch's membrane. These leaky vessels cause subretinal fluid, hemorrhage, and rapid photoreceptor death.

The strongest modifiable cause — current smokers carry 2-4 fold higher risk of advanced AMD versus never-smokers, with dose-response by pack-years. Smoking accelerates oxidative damage, reduces macular pigment, and lowers serum antioxidants.

Long-term high blue-light exposure may contribute by generating reactive oxygen species in photoreceptors. Evidence is mixed and effect size smaller than smoking, but lifetime outdoor sun exposure correlates with AMD severity in several cohorts.
Heritability of advanced AMD is approximately 70%. Beyond CFH, well-characterized loci include ARMS2/HTRA1 on chromosome 10, which roughly doubles risk per allele in homozygotes.

Hypertension, high serum LDL, and obesity correlate with AMD progression — likely through shared endothelial dysfunction and choroidal vascular insufficiency. Cardiovascular disease and AMD share risk pathways.

riskFactors.description: Prevalence rises from 2% at 50-59 to over 30% by age 80; almost all clinically meaningful AMD presents after 60.
Late AMD is roughly 2.5x more prevalent in Europeans than in East Asians and Africans (Wong et al, Lancet Global Health 2014).

riskFactors.factor: Family history of AMD in a first-degree relative
riskFactors.description: Sibling-recurrence studies show 3-6 fold higher risk; the CFH and ARMS2 variants account for a large fraction of familial clustering.
Most important modifiable risk factor — 2-4x increased risk of advanced AMD; risk falls within 5-10 years of quitting but never returns fully to baseline.
Later-life female prevalence is higher than in men, partly because women live longer; postmenopausal estrogen loss may also contribute.

riskFactors.factor: Diet low in green leafy vegetables and oily fish
riskFactors.description: Lower dietary lutein/zeaxanthin and omega-3 intake correlate with higher AMD incidence in cohort studies; AREDS2 supplementation reduces 5-year progression risk by 25%.
Obese adults have 1.5-2x higher risk of late AMD versus normal-weight peers; regular physical activity is associated with lower progression risk.
Elevated blood pressure raises choroidal vascular resistance and is linked to faster AMD progression in longitudinal data.
Blue or light-colored irises offer less macular protection from oxidative light damage; modest but consistent epidemiologic association.
Highly hyperopic eyes show modestly higher AMD incidence, likely reflecting choroidal geometry differences.

affectedDemographics: Approximately 20 million US adults have some form of AMD; about 1.5 million have vision-threatening late AMD (CDC 2019)
Globally projected to affect 288 million people by 2040 (Wong et al, Lancet Global Health 2014)
European-ancestry populations have the highest late-AMD prevalence; East Asian populations have higher rates of polypoidal choroidal vasculopathy (a wet AMD variant)
Women account for roughly 60% of late AMD cases in the US, partly because of longer life expectancy
Highest national prevalence rates are reported in Iceland, Norway, and parts of the US Midwest in adults over 75
diagnosisOverview: AMD diagnosis is made on a dilated retinal exam supplemented by optical coherence tomography (OCT), with imaging chosen based on whether dry or wet disease is suspected. The clinician begins with visual acuity, an Amsler grid screen for metamorphopsia or scotomas, and a dilated fundus exam looking for drusen, RPE pigment changes, geographic atrophy, hemorrhage, or subretinal fluid. Spectral-domain OCT is now standard for all suspected AMD — it produces a cross-section of the retina that shows drusen volume, RPE elevations, intraretinal and subretinal fluid, and atrophy with micron-level resolution. Fundus autofluorescence highlights areas of RPE dysfunction and is the most sensitive tool for measuring geographic atrophy progression. Fluorescein angiography or OCT angiography is performed when wet AMD is suspected, to localize and characterize the choroidal neovascular membrane and guide anti-VEGF therapy. The American Academy of Ophthalmology 2024 Preferred Practice Pattern recommends OCT at every visit for monitoring intermediate and advanced AMD, and home Amsler-grid or smartphone-based hyperacuity testing between visits to catch wet conversion early. Important differentials include diabetic macular edema, central serous chorioretinopathy, vitreomacular traction, myopic maculopathy, and macular dystrophies — most are distinguished readily on OCT. Genetic testing is not recommended in routine care but may be considered in atypical young-onset cases or where family screening is requested.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Direct visualization of drusen, pigmentary changes, geographic atrophy, hemorrhage, and exudate. Still the foundational exam for AMD diagnosis and staging.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Drops are used to widen the pupil over 20-30 minutes. The clinician then examines the retina with a slit-lamp and lens. Vision is blurry and light-sensitive for 3-6 hours after dilation; bring sunglasses and avoid driving immediately afterward.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Cross-sectional retinal imaging that detects drusen volume, subretinal or intraretinal fluid, geographic atrophy, and pigment epithelial detachments. Repeated to monitor progression and response to therapy.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Painless laser-light scan taking 1-3 minutes per eye while the patient fixes on a target. No dye injection, no contact with the eye, no recovery time.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects metamorphopsia and central scotomas. Used both at diagnosis and as a daily home tool to catch new wet AMD activity between clinic visits.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The patient covers one eye and looks at the center of a grid of horizontal and vertical lines at reading distance, noting any wavy lines, missing squares, or blurred areas. Takes under a minute per eye.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Maps lipofuscin distribution and identifies areas of RPE atrophy. The most sensitive technique for measuring geographic atrophy lesion size and progression rate.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A specialized camera takes brief, bright flash photos. Pupil dilation is usually needed. No injection, takes a few minutes per eye.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Non-invasive 3D imaging of retinal and choroidal vasculature; detects choroidal neovascularization without dye injection. Increasingly used as first-line where available.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A longer OCT scan, typically 3-6 minutes per eye, requiring steady fixation. No dye, no needle, no recovery time.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold-standard for confirming and classifying choroidal neovascular membranes when OCT-A is inconclusive or before initial anti-VEGF therapy.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small amount of fluorescein dye is injected into an arm vein, and serial fundus photos are taken over 5-10 minutes. Skin and urine turn yellow-orange for 24 hours. Severe allergic reactions are rare (under 1 in 200,000).

diagnosticTests.test: Visual acuity and contrast sensitivity testing
diagnosticTests.purpose: Establishes baseline central vision and tracks functional change. Contrast sensitivity often declines before letter acuity in early AMD.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard letter-chart testing at distance and near, with best correction. Contrast tests use progressively fainter letters. Takes 5-10 minutes per eye.

treatmentOverview: AMD treatment depends on stage and form. Early AMD has no proven pharmacologic therapy; care focuses on smoking cessation, cardiovascular risk control, and Mediterranean-style diet rich in green leafy vegetables and oily fish. Intermediate AMD or late AMD in one eye is the indication for the AREDS2 oral supplement formulation (lutein 10 mg, zeaxanthin 2 mg, vitamin C 500 mg, vitamin E 400 IU, zinc 80 mg, copper 2 mg), which reduces the 5-year risk of progression to advanced AMD by approximately 25%. Wet AMD is treated with intravitreal anti-VEGF injections — aflibercept, ranibizumab, bevacizumab (used off-label), brolucizumab, and faricimab — administered monthly to every 16 weeks based on treat-and-extend protocols. The CATT trial established that bevacizumab is non-inferior to ranibizumab for visual acuity outcomes at one year, and aflibercept 8 mg and faricimab now offer extended-interval dosing in many patients. The treatment goal in wet AMD is to dry the macula on OCT and preserve vision; on average, treated patients gain or maintain visual acuity for years, whereas untreated wet AMD loses 3-4 lines within 24 months. Geographic atrophy now has two FDA-approved complement inhibitors — pegcetacoplan (intravitreal, approved 2023) and avacincaptad pegol (approved 2023) — which slow lesion growth by roughly 14-27% but do not restore lost vision. Low-vision rehabilitation, optical and electronic magnification, and orientation training are integrated throughout to maintain reading, driving, and independence. The AAO 2024 Preferred Practice Pattern is the operative guideline for current US practice.

medicalTreatments.name: Aflibercept (Eylea) 2 mg or 8 mg intravitreal injection
medicalTreatments.description: VEGF-A and placental growth factor decoy receptor. Standard induction is three monthly injections followed by treat-and-extend; the 8 mg formulation allows extension up to 16 weeks in many patients.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Mean letter gain of 8-10 ETDRS letters at 1 year in wet AMD; 90% of treated eyes maintain or improve vision.

medicalTreatments.name: Faricimab (Vabysmo) 6 mg intravitreal injection
medicalTreatments.description: Bispecific antibody targeting both VEGF-A and angiopoietin-2. Approved 2022 for wet AMD; supports extended dosing intervals up to 16 weeks.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Non-inferior visual acuity gain versus aflibercept in TENAYA/LUCERNE trials; ~45% of patients maintain Q16-week dosing at 2 years.

medicalTreatments.name: Ranibizumab (Lucentis) 0.5 mg intravitreal injection
medicalTreatments.description: Humanized anti-VEGF antibody fragment. First anti-VEGF approved for wet AMD (2006). Monthly or treat-and-extend dosing.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Mean gain of 6.8-8.5 letters at 1 year in CATT (PMID 21526923); equivalent efficacy to bevacizumab.

medicalTreatments.name: Bevacizumab (Avastin) 1.25 mg intravitreal injection — off-label
medicalTreatments.description: Anti-VEGF antibody used widely off-label for wet AMD. Compounded for ophthalmic use; substantially cheaper than branded agents and standard of care in many public health systems.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Non-inferior to ranibizumab in the CATT trial (5.9-8.0 letter gain at 1 year).

medicalTreatments.name: Pegcetacoplan (Syfovre) intravitreal injection
medicalTreatments.description: Complement C3 inhibitor approved 2023 for geographic atrophy from AMD. Given every 25-60 days. Does not restore vision but slows lesion growth.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces geographic atrophy lesion growth by approximately 17-20% over 2 years in DERBY/OAKS trials.

medicalTreatments.name: Avacincaptad pegol (Izervay) intravitreal injection
medicalTreatments.description: Complement C5 inhibitor approved 2023 for geographic atrophy. Monthly dosing for up to 12 months.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces GA lesion growth by roughly 14-27% at 18 months versus sham (GATHER1, GATHER2 trials).

medicalTreatments.name: AREDS2 oral supplement formulation
medicalTreatments.description: Daily oral formulation: lutein 10 mg, zeaxanthin 2 mg, vitamin C 500 mg, vitamin E 400 IU, zinc 80 mg (or 25 mg), copper 2 mg. Indicated in intermediate AMD or late AMD in one eye.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces 5-year risk of progression to advanced AMD by approximately 25% (AREDS, PMID 23644932).

medicalTreatments.name: Brolucizumab (Beovu) 6 mg intravitreal injection
medicalTreatments.description: Single-chain anti-VEGF antibody fragment offering deeper drying of the retina and Q12-week maintenance. Carries higher rates of intraocular inflammation than alternatives.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Non-inferior to aflibercept at week 48 (HAWK/HARRIER); roughly 50% of patients maintained Q12-week dosing.

surgicalOptions.name: Submacular hemorrhage displacement (pneumatic displacement with tPA)
surgicalOptions.description: For large submacular hemorrhages from wet AMD, an intravitreal injection of tissue plasminogen activator combined with an expansile gas bubble displaces the blood away from the fovea, allowing anti-VEGF therapy and visual recovery.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Successful displacement in 70-85% of cases; meaningful visual recovery in roughly half.

surgicalOptions.name: Implantable miniature telescope (CentraSight)
surgicalOptions.description: An intraocular telescope implanted in one eye after cataract removal for patients with end-stage AMD and bilateral central scotomas. Magnifies central images onto healthier peripheral retina.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Approximately 75% of implanted patients gain 3 or more lines of distance or near vision; rigorous candidate selection required.

alternativeTreatments.name: Mediterranean-style diet pattern
alternativeTreatments.description: Cohort data (Coimbra Eye Study, AREDS post-hoc) show roughly 40% lower risk of late AMD progression with high adherence to a Mediterranean dietary pattern rich in vegetables, oily fish, nuts, and olive oil.

alternativeTreatments.name: Smoking cessation
alternativeTreatments.description: Quitting smoking lowers AMD progression risk over 5-10 years; combined with AREDS2 and cardiovascular risk reduction, this is the highest-yield lifestyle intervention.

whySeeSpecialist: A retina specialist (a vitreoretinal-trained ophthalmologist) should be involved as soon as wet AMD is suspected, when geographic atrophy is identified, when anti-VEGF therapy is being considered, or when there is diagnostic uncertainty. General ophthalmologists and optometrists screen and monitor early and intermediate AMD; retina referral is urgent (within 1-2 weeks) for any new central distortion or sudden vision change.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for board-certified ophthalmologists with vitreoretinal fellowship training and high-volume anti-VEGF injection experience. Confirm the practice has on-site OCT and either OCT-angiography or fluorescein angiography. Continuity matters — wet AMD treatment is an indefinite multi-year relationship of injections every 4-16 weeks, so accessibility and scheduling reliability are practical criteria worth weighing.
preventionStrategies: Stop smoking — the single highest-yield action; risk reduction begins within 5 years of cessation
Eat a Mediterranean-style diet with daily green leafy vegetables, oily fish twice weekly, and nuts; associated with up to 40% lower late AMD risk
Maintain healthy blood pressure, LDL cholesterol, and weight — AMD shares vascular risk pathways with stroke and coronary disease
Wear UV- and blue-light-blocking sunglasses outdoors, especially at high altitude, in snow, or on water
Take AREDS2 supplements only if intermediate AMD or unilateral late AMD is documented — they are not recommended for primary prevention in normal eyes
Get a dilated eye exam every 1-2 years from age 60, or annually from any age with a first-degree relative diagnosed with AMD
lifestyleModifications: Check an Amsler grid daily or use a smartphone hyperacuity app — one eye at a time, same lighting, same distance — and report new distortion within 48 hours
Improve home lighting: 100-watt-equivalent LED task lights for reading, high-contrast labels on appliances, and avoid harsh glare
Use large-print materials, audiobooks, screen magnification, and electronic readers (Kindle, iPad) with adjustable font size
Continue daily exercise — at least 150 minutes of moderate activity weekly; physical activity slows AMD progression independently of weight
Limit alcohol to moderate levels; heavy alcohol intake is associated with faster drusen accumulation in some cohorts
Treat sleep apnea, hypertension, and dyslipidemia aggressively — shared vascular pathways accelerate retinal injury
Engage low-vision rehabilitation early once central acuity drops below 20/40; do not wait for severe loss

recommended: Dark leafy greens (kale, spinach, collards) — high in lutein and zeaxanthin, the carotenoids that concentrate in the macula
Oily fish 2-3 times weekly (salmon, sardines, mackerel) for omega-3 fatty acids (DHA/EPA)
Brightly colored vegetables and fruits (carrots, sweet potato, peppers, berries) for mixed antioxidants
Nuts and seeds (walnuts, almonds, flaxseed) and extra-virgin olive oil for vitamin E and unsaturated fats
Whole grains, legumes, and pulses as protein and fiber sources
Citrus and bell peppers for vitamin C — daily intake correlates with lower AMD progression
avoid: Heavy red and processed meats — high intake correlates with faster AMD progression in the Blue Mountains Eye Study
Refined carbohydrates with high glycemic index (white bread, white rice, sugary drinks) — higher dietary glycemic index is linked to higher late-AMD risk
Trans fats and deep-fried foods — promote oxidative stress in the retina
Excessive alcohol — more than 3 drinks daily associated with accelerated drusen progression
Cigarette smoke (active or secondhand) — not a food, but the single most important dietary-adjacent exposure to eliminate

exerciseGuidelines: Regular moderate-intensity aerobic activity (walking, swimming, cycling) for at least 150 minutes per week is associated with lower AMD progression risk in cohort data. Resistance training twice weekly supports overall metabolic health. There are no specific exercise restrictions after intravitreal injection beyond avoiding swimming, hot tubs, and eye rubbing for 24-48 hours.
dailyManagement: Perform a daily Amsler grid check (or smartphone hyperacuity test) one eye at a time and report any new distortion within 48 hours
Keep all scheduled retina follow-up and OCT appointments — missed injections in wet AMD are the strongest predictor of vision loss
Take the AREDS2 supplement at the same time daily if indicated; consistency matters more than time of day
Maintain blood pressure, LDL cholesterol, and HbA1c in target ranges through the primary care team
Use bright, glare-controlled lighting for reading and detailed tasks; keep an emergency light source by the bed
Plan transportation alternatives if vision impairs night driving; voluntary driving restriction protects independence longer than a forced loss of license
prognosis: Most patients with early or intermediate AMD retain reading and driving vision for the rest of their lives — only about 18% progress to late AMD within 5 years, and progression is slower with AREDS2 supplementation and smoking cessation. Untreated wet AMD typically causes severe central vision loss (legal blindness, 20/200 or worse, in the affected eye) within 1-2 years; with timely anti-VEGF therapy, 30-40% of patients gain 3 or more lines of vision at 1 year and roughly 90% maintain or improve vision. Geographic atrophy progresses inexorably at 1.5-2.6 mm² per year on average; the new complement inhibitors slow but do not stop this. AMD does not cause complete blindness — peripheral vision is preserved, allowing mobility and many adaptive activities. The strongest predictors of good outcomes are early detection of wet AMD, adherence to injection schedules, smoking cessation, and engagement with low-vision rehabilitation when central acuity falls below 20/40.
recoveryTimeline: Anti-VEGF injection effects appear within days — vision often stabilizes by week 1-2 and may improve over 3-12 months of regular treatment. Most patients need an induction series (typically three monthly injections) followed by treat-and-extend maintenance for years. Geographic atrophy treatment with complement inhibitors slows progression but does not produce visible improvement; benefit is measured by OCT and autofluorescence over 12-24 months. Low-vision rehabilitation typically delivers measurable functional gains in reading speed and independence within 6-8 weeks of starting structured training.
complications: Severe central vision loss and legal blindness (20/200 or worse) in untreated or treatment-refractory wet AMD — preventable with timely anti-VEGF therapy
Bilateral geographic atrophy producing dense central scotomas that prevent reading and recognizing faces — current therapies only slow progression
Submacular hemorrhage from wet AMD, which can cause sudden severe vision loss and may require gas-and-tPA displacement surgery
Disciform scar — end-stage fibrotic scar in the macula after long-standing untreated or poorly controlled wet AMD
Falls and fractures — central vision loss roughly doubles fall risk in adults over 75; hip fractures are a leading cause of mortality in this group
Depression and social isolation — clinically significant depression occurs in 25-30% of patients with bilateral late AMD; screening and treatment improve quality of life
Endophthalmitis or retinal detachment from intravitreal injections — rare (roughly 1 in 2,000-5,000 injections) but serious

supportResources.name: American Academy of Ophthalmology — EyeSmart AMD
supportResources.description: Authoritative US patient education on AMD from the body that writes the practice guideline.

supportResources.name: National Eye Institute — AMD
supportResources.description: NIH patient-facing overview with AREDS/AREDS2 evidence and self-care resources.

supportResources.name: Macular Society (UK)
supportResources.description: Patient charity providing helpline support, peer groups, and low-vision guidance.

supportResources.name: American Macular Degeneration Foundation
supportResources.description: US patient organization with educational tools and an Amsler grid download.

confusedWithConditions.name: Diabetic Retinopathy
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Diabetic retinopathy affects the entire retina with microaneurysms, dot-blot hemorrhages, and (in proliferative disease) new vessels at the disc or elsewhere — not drusen. It requires diabetes as a precondition and typically affects younger patients than AMD. Both can coexist.

confusedWithConditions.name: Glaucoma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Glaucoma damages the optic nerve and causes peripheral (not central) vision loss with elevated intraocular pressure and characteristic optic disc cupping. AMD preserves peripheral vision and primarily affects the central macula.

confusedWithConditions.name: Cataracts
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Cataracts cloud the natural lens, producing diffuse blur, glare, and color desaturation that improves dramatically with surgery. AMD affects the retina behind a clear lens; if vision does not improve after cataract surgery, AMD or another retinal condition is suspected.

confusedWithConditions.name: Retinal Detachment
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Retinal detachment causes sudden flashes, floaters, and a curtain-like loss of peripheral vision — distinct from the gradual or distortion-based central loss of AMD. It is an emergency that requires urgent surgical reattachment.

relatedConditions.name: Macular Degeneration (overview)
relatedConditions.relevance: Broader category page covering all macular degenerative diseases including AMD and juvenile forms.

relatedConditions.name: Diabetic Retinopathy
relatedConditions.relevance: Other leading cause of vision loss in adults; shares anti-VEGF therapy and OCT monitoring pathways.

relatedConditions.name: Glaucoma
relatedConditions.relevance: Frequently coexists in elderly patients; combined disease accelerates functional vision loss.

relatedConditions.name: Cataracts
relatedConditions.relevance: Common co-existing condition; cataract surgery does not worsen AMD and may improve overall vision.

relatedConditions.name: High Blood Pressure
relatedConditions.relevance: Shared vascular risk pathway; uncontrolled hypertension accelerates AMD progression.

faqs.question: Can age-related macular degeneration be cured?
faqs.answer: AMD cannot be cured, but it can be controlled. Anti-VEGF injections preserve or improve vision in 90% of treated wet AMD patients. AREDS2 supplements slow progression in intermediate dry AMD by about 25% over 5 years. New complement inhibitors slow geographic atrophy. Damage already done to photoreceptors cannot be reversed, which is why early diagnosis and treatment matter.

faqs.question: What is the difference between wet and dry AMD?
faqs.answer: Dry AMD (85% of cases) progresses slowly through drusen and atrophy over years. Wet AMD (15%) involves abnormal new blood vessels growing under the retina, leaking fluid or blood, and can erase central vision within weeks. Wet AMD is treated with intravitreal anti-VEGF injections; dry AMD is managed with diet, AREDS2 supplements, and (in geographic atrophy) complement inhibitor injections.

faqs.question: Will I go completely blind from AMD?
faqs.answer: No. AMD damages central vision but preserves peripheral vision, so it does not cause total blindness. Even patients with advanced bilateral AMD retain enough peripheral vision to walk safely. Severe AMD does meet the legal definition of blindness (visual acuity 20/200 or worse) and impairs reading, driving, and face recognition.

faqs.question: What are the first signs of AMD?
faqs.answer: The earliest symptoms are subtle blurring of central vision, difficulty reading small print under normal light, and slow dark-adaptation. The hallmark warning sign is metamorphopsia — straight lines appearing wavy or distorted, often noticed when one eye is covered. Sudden new distortion in one eye suggests wet AMD and requires retina specialist evaluation within days.

faqs.question: How is AMD diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is made by a dilated retinal exam with optical coherence tomography (OCT) of the macula. The eye doctor looks for drusen, pigmentary changes, geographic atrophy, or signs of wet disease such as subretinal fluid. OCT angiography or fluorescein angiography may be added when wet AMD is suspected. Visual acuity, contrast sensitivity, and Amsler grid testing complete the workup.

faqs.question: Do AREDS2 vitamins work for AMD?
faqs.answer: Yes, but only in specific situations. The AREDS2 oral formula (lutein 10 mg, zeaxanthin 2 mg, vitamin C 500 mg, vitamin E 400 IU, zinc 80 mg, copper 2 mg) reduces the 5-year risk of progression to advanced AMD by about 25% in people with intermediate AMD or late AMD in one eye. It does not help people with no AMD or with early AMD only, and it does not restore lost vision.

faqs.question: Are anti-VEGF injections painful?
faqs.answer: Most patients describe intravitreal injections as briefly uncomfortable rather than painful. The eye is numbed with anesthetic drops or gel, cleaned with iodine, and the injection itself takes less than a second. Mild grittiness, redness, or floaters for 1-2 days afterward are normal. Severe pain, worsening redness, or vision drop after an injection should be reported immediately to rule out endophthalmitis.

faqs.question: How often will I need eye injections for wet AMD?
faqs.answer: Most patients receive three monthly induction injections, then continue on a treat-and-extend protocol that lengthens intervals to 6, 8, 12, or 16 weeks based on response. Some patients need monthly injections indefinitely; others can extend to 12-16 weeks with newer agents such as aflibercept 8 mg or faricimab. Treatment is generally lifelong, with intervals individualized by OCT findings.

faqs.question: Is AMD hereditary?
faqs.answer: There is a strong genetic component — heritability of advanced AMD is approximately 70%. Variants in CFH (complement factor H) and ARMS2/HTRA1 each substantially increase risk. A first-degree relative with AMD raises personal risk 3-6 fold. Routine genetic testing is not recommended; family history should prompt earlier and more frequent dilated eye exams.

faqs.question: Can quitting smoking reverse AMD?
faqs.answer: Quitting smoking does not reverse damage already done, but it substantially slows further progression. Risk of advanced AMD falls steadily over 5-10 years after quitting, though it never returns fully to never-smoker baseline. Smoking cessation is the single highest-yield modifiable intervention for AMD and is recommended at every visit by the AAO 2024 guidelines.

faqs.question: What is geographic atrophy?
faqs.answer: Geographic atrophy is a form of advanced dry AMD with well-demarcated patches of retinal pigment epithelium and photoreceptor loss. It progresses at 1.5-2.6 mm² per year and causes dense central scotomas once it involves the fovea. Two FDA-approved complement inhibitors (pegcetacoplan, avacincaptad pegol) slow GA lesion growth by 14-27% but do not restore vision.

faqs.question: Does cataract surgery worsen AMD?
faqs.answer: No. Large cohort studies and randomized data show cataract surgery does not accelerate AMD progression and typically improves overall vision in patients with both conditions. A clear lens makes monitoring AMD easier and improves the patient's ability to use remaining central vision. AMD should not be a contraindication to indicated cataract surgery.

faqs.question: How quickly does AMD progress?
faqs.answer: Progression varies widely. Early AMD has only a 1-2% chance of progressing to late AMD over 5 years. Intermediate AMD progresses to advanced disease in about 18% of patients over 5 years. Wet AMD, once active, can cause severe vision loss within weeks if untreated. Dry geographic atrophy typically expands over 2-5 years before affecting central vision.

faqs.question: Can diet alone prevent AMD?
faqs.answer: Diet meaningfully lowers risk but cannot eliminate it. A Mediterranean-style pattern rich in green leafy vegetables, oily fish, nuts, and olive oil is associated with up to 40% lower late AMD risk in cohort studies. Diet works alongside not-smoking, blood pressure control, exercise, and (where indicated) AREDS2 supplements; the combination is far more effective than any single intervention.

faqs.question: How much does AMD treatment cost?
faqs.answer: In the US, branded anti-VEGF injections range from roughly USD 1,800-2,200 per dose for aflibercept or faricimab; compounded bevacizumab is much cheaper at USD 50-100. Medicare and most commercial insurers cover medically necessary injections, often leaving a 20% coinsurance. AREDS2 supplements cost USD 15-30 per month. Out-of-pocket costs depend heavily on coverage and country.

faqs.question: Can I still drive with AMD?
faqs.answer: Many patients with early or intermediate AMD continue driving safely. Driving requires meeting state visual acuity and field standards (typically 20/40 corrected vision in the better eye in most US states). As AMD advances, daytime driving is usually given up before night driving, and many patients voluntarily restrict to familiar local routes. An occupational therapy driving assessment is helpful when in doubt.

faqs.question: What is an Amsler grid?
faqs.answer: An Amsler grid is a square pattern of horizontal and vertical lines with a central dot, used to detect distortion or missing spots in central vision. Patients with AMD use it daily — one eye at a time at reading distance — and report any new wavy lines, missing squares, or dark spots within 48 hours. Free printable grids are available from major eye charities and AAO.

faqs.question: Do blue-light glasses prevent AMD?
faqs.answer: Evidence does not support blue-light blocking lenses for AMD prevention. Indoor digital screens emit far less blue light than sunlight and have not been linked to AMD. UV-blocking sunglasses outdoors are sensible for general eye health. The strongest modifiable interventions remain smoking cessation, Mediterranean diet, and cardiovascular risk control.

faqs.question: When should I see a retina specialist for AMD?
faqs.answer: See a retina specialist urgently (within 1-2 weeks) for any new central distortion, blurred spot, or sudden vision drop in one eye — possible wet AMD activation. Routine retina referral is appropriate for intermediate AMD, geographic atrophy, atypical findings, or when anti-VEGF therapy is being considered. General ophthalmologists and optometrists manage early and intermediate AMD between specialist visits.

faqs.question: Are stem cell treatments available for AMD?
faqs.answer: Stem cell therapies for AMD remain investigational. Early clinical trials of retinal pigment epithelium transplantation have shown safety and modest visual gains in geographic atrophy, but no stem cell treatment is FDA-approved for routine use. Patients should be cautious about unregulated overseas clinics offering unproven stem cell injections — multiple cases of severe vision loss have been reported.

faqs.question: Can children get AMD?
faqs.answer: Classic age-related macular degeneration is rare under age 50. Children and young adults with central vision loss and macular changes usually have an inherited macular dystrophy such as Stargardt disease or Best disease, not AMD. These conditions require genetic testing and management by inherited retinal disease specialists, with different prognoses and treatment pathways.
